coredata.dc:title: Selective detection of nitenpyram by silica-supported carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Rostami, Masoumeh
Zhang, Baiyu
Zhang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a fluorescent sensor of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and silica gel hybrid was developed for the quantitative detection of nitenpyram, a toxic neonicotinoid existing in groundwater and/or surface water.The prepared N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor exhibited remarkable sensing selectivity and sensitivity towards nitenpyram among the four pesticides and six metal ions. A prominent fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs@SiO2 at 445 nm was observed in the presence of nitenpyram with a linear response range of 0–300.0 mg L−1 and an estimated limit of detection of 1.53 mg L−1. The main cause for selective sensing is that nitenpyram absorbs the excitation light of N-CQDs@SiO2, leading to fluorescence quenching of the sensor through the inner filter effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Nitenpyram
Inner filter effect

Detection of NTP in natural water samples
pH effect on fluorescent sensing of NTP
Fluorescent sensing of NTP by N-CQDs@SiO2
Selective sensing of NTP and its mechanism
Fluorescence sensing of NTP in natural water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image (a), FTIR spectrum (b), survey XPS spectrum (c), high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (d), N 1s (e), O 1s (f), UV–Vis absorbance, fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of N-CQDs (g), the Tauc plot (h) of N-CQDs.
FT-IR (a), survey XPS (b), and UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence excitation/emission (c) spectra of N-CQDs@SiO2.
pH effect on the fluorescence intensity and sensitivity of N-CQDs@SiO2.
Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs@SiO2 at different concentrations of NTP (a), the linear correlation of FL intensity variation versus the concentration of NTP (b).
FL intensity variation of N-CQDs@SiO2 by various pesticides (a), FL intensity variation of N-CQDs@SiO2 in different metal ion solutions (b). (DI water as a solvent for all solutions).

UV–vis absorbance of four pesticides and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of N-CQDs@SiO2 (a), the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs@SiO2 in the absence and presence of NTP (b), reduction cyclic voltammogram of N-CQDs (c), schematic diagram of the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by NTP through PET (d).
Performance comparison with other fluorometry-based methods for NTP detection.
Detection of NTP from natural water samples.

head.ce:title: Selective detection of nitenpyram by silica-supported carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Process Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s A1C 5S7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Process Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s A1C 5S7, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Northern Region Persistent Organic Pollution Control (NRPOP) Laboratory, Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s A1C 5S7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Northern Region Persistent Organic Pollution Control (NRPOP) Laboratory
Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Northern Region Persistent Organic Pollution (NRPOP) Control Laboratory, Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s A1C 5S7, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a fluorescent sensor of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and silica gel hybrid was developed for the quantitative detection of nitenpyram, a toxic neonicotinoid existing in groundwater and/or surface water.The prepared N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor exhibited remarkable sensing selectivity and sensitivity towards nitenpyram among the four pesticides and six metal ions. A prominent fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs@SiO2 at 445 nm was observed in the presence of nitenpyram with a linear response range of 0–300.0 mg L−1 and an estimated limit of detection of 1.53 mg L−1. The main cause for selective sensing is that nitenpyram absorbs the excitation light of N-CQDs@SiO2, leading to fluorescence quenching of the sensor through the inner filter effect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Neonicotinoid pesticides, including acetamiprid, thiacloprid, nitenpyram, imidacloprid, clothianidin, thiamethoxam, and dinotefuran, constitute a major class of pesticides that are widely used in agriculture to protect crops from pests [1]. Neonicotinoid is highly toxic to insects but not to mammals, birds, or other higher organisms [2,3]. However, there is evidence that long-term exposure to these substances can have an impact on mammals and even on human health [1–4]. Neonicotinoids are water-soluble, persistent chemicals that have built up over time in the environment [5]. Studies have shown that between 80 and 90 % of neonicotinoids are transported from agricultural fields into the surface and groundwater [5–7]. This raises the concern that these compounds may contaminate natural sources of drinking water. It is therefore important to wisely monitor and control their existence in the environment by using reliable quantitative detection methods [8,9].

The quantification of trace amounts of neonicotinoids can currently be done using traditional analytical techniques such as gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and GC/HPLC-MS, but these methods are expensive, time-consuming, and require sample preparation [10–12]. To overcome these limitations, rapid sensing technologies were developed based on different transduction principles (e.g., variations in Raman scattering, fluorescence signal, refractive index, and electrical conductivity) [12]. Among these methods, fluorescence-based sensing offers the advantages of high selectivity, simplicity, rapidity, and low cost [13,14]. The development and application of fluorescence sensors for detecting and monitoring analytes relevant to environmental contamination have attracted increasing attention [15].

Due to their remarkable structural and functional attributes, nanomaterials have been the focus of intense and extensive study in the development of numerous fluorescence sensing technologies [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials exhibit attractive properties of tunable fluorescence, high stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and simple synthesis, and thus have great potential to replace hazardous metallic quantum dots for sensing [17]. CQDs (less than 10 nm in particle size) are volumetric mixtures of graphitic and turbostratic carbons with high nitrogen and oxygen concentrations [18,19]. Due to their structural diversity, CQDs exhibit good sensitivity and selectivity for a variety of analytes [13,20–24]. Although a variety of CQDs with different chemical structures have been developed, the fluorescence origin of CQDs is mainly attributed to the quantum confinement effect (determined by conjugated core), surface state (by surface functional groups), and molecular fluorescence [23,25,26]. Interaction of CQDs with analytes leads to fluorescence change (intensity, wavelength, or lifetime) of CQDs, which is utilized for the detection of various analytes [17,27].

Different mechanisms, such as photo-induced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and the inner filter effect (IFE) may contribute to the fluorescent sensing process [12,17,28]. PET is a deactivation process involving a direct electron transfer between an excited fluorescer (CQDs) and an analyte able to donate or accept electrons [12,28]. FRET involves non-radiative energy transfer through long-range dipole–dipole interactions between the donor–acceptor pair [29]. A spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the energy donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor is required in FRET [29]. Whereas, IFE occurs due to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by the quencher in a detection system when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the excitation and/or emission spectra of CQDs [30,31].

The detection of neonicotinoids using CQDs-based fluorescent sensors has been studied by several research groups [32–37]. CQDs synthesized from gelatin were used as fluorescence sensors for the detection of five pesticides (imidacloprid, atrazine, chlorpyrifos, lindane, and tetradifon) [32]. The prepared CQDs displayed different binding characteristics with pesticides, resulting in a turn-on effect (30.0 % increase) of fluorescence in the presence of imidacloprid, and a turn-off effect (35.0 % quenching) of fluorescence in the presence of tetradifon [32]. Gao et al. reported an N-(aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol) functionalized CQDs sensor for the fluorescence sensing of thiamethoxam, flubendiamide, dimethoate, dipterex, and chlorpyrifos [33]. 15 % and 8 % of fluorescence quenching were observed when the sensor was in contact with chlorpyrifos and thiamethoxam, respectively. Most recently, fluorescent CQDs-metal nanocluster (NC) hybrids and aptamer-mediated CQD biosensors were developed to increase the sensing selectivity and sensitivity [35–37]. A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe of sulfur-doped CQDs and CuNC hybrid was introduced for sensitive and selective detection of dinotefuran. The as-prepared sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity and analytical performance of dinotefuran with a limit of detection (LOD) of 7.04 μM [35]. An aptamer-mediated CQDs biosensor was reported by Jiao et al. for selective sensing of acetamiprid from complex sample matrices [36]. In this case, the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched once aptamer was wrapped around them. Upon the addition of acetamiprid, the preferential interaction between aptamer and acetamiprid caused the release of CQDs and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs recovered linearly with the concentration of acetamiprid. The sensor provided extremely low LOD of 0.04, 0.15, and 0.11 ng/L (acetamiprid) in an aqueous buffer, spiked human serum samples, and lettuce samples, respectively [36]. Results from these studies show that the selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs-based fluorescent sensors highly depend on the type of binding and binding constants between neonicotinoid pesticides and CQDs. Nonetheless, the difficulty in identifying the recognition components and the uncertainty of the precise fluorescence mechanisms of CQDs continue to be major obstacles in the development and use of CQDs-based fluorescent sensors. More research efforts should be dedicated to developing neonicotinoid-specific detection using CQDs-based fluorescent sensors.

In this study, a turn-off silica-supported CQDs sensor was fabricated for the selective and sensitive detection of nitenpyram (NTP), a neonicotinoid pesticide still being used commercially. Silica gel was selected as the immobilization carrier for CQDs in this work due to its protective characteristics of the porous structure, low cost, and non-toxicity [38]. The as-prepared sensor was characterized by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Its fluorescence responses to several pesticide molecules and metal ions were investigated and the results indicated that the prepared sensor showed very good sensing selectivity and sensitivity to NTP through the mechanism of IFE.

The anhydrous citric acid (99.5 %) and ethylenediamine (99 %) from Fisher Scientific, and ACS grade anhydrous ethanol from Greenfield were used to synthesize nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 99 %), LUDOX-HS 40 colloidal silica, and all pesticides (analytical standard) including nitenpyram (NTP), imidacloprid (IMD), thiamethoxam (TMX), and atrazine (ATZ) were purchased from Sigma. ACS grade metal ions salts were obtained from Fisher Scientific. These chemicals were used as received without further purification. If it is not particularly indicated, all solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water from a Milli-Q water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N-CQDs@SiO2 fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of N-CQDs was performed by the hydrothermal/solvothermal method. Briefly, 0.965 g of ethylenediamine and 0.84 g of citric acid were mixed with 20.0 mL of ethanol-H2O solution (1:3 v/v) in a Teflon-lined autoclave at room temperature and then transferred into the oven at 200 °C for 3.0 h. The resultant products were allowed to cool down at room temperature. The obtained solution was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 0.5 h and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters to remove any large particles. Unreacted chemicals were also removed using a dialysis (molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da) process for 24 h, and the solid N-CQDs were collected after freeze-drying.

To immobilize N-CQDs onto the silica gel, 5.0 mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) solution was mixed with 5.0 mL of colloidal silica LUDOX-HS 40 in acidic conditions (pH 2.0) adjusted by adding drops of HCl. The mixture was then neutralized by adding NaOH solution to pH 7.0 [39]. In the next step, N-CQDs@SiO2 gel was prepared by mixing 10.0 mL of the as-prepared silica solution with 8.0 mL of the N-CQDs solution (1.0 mg/mL). The mixture was ultrasound for 1.0 h and stirred overnight. The resultant solution can be used as the sensor.

The as-prepared N-CQDs and N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor were characterized using various apparatus. The FTIR spectra were obtained from a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) over a range of wavelengths from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The HR-TEM images of N-CQDs were taken on an FEI Titan 80–300 HB electron microscope (FEI, USA). XPS measurements were performed using an XPS-PHI 5000 VPIII (ULPAC-PHI Inc., Japan) with peak positions being recalibrated by shifting C 1s peak to 284.8 eV. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor with and without analytes were obtained from a PTI-Quanta Master spectrofluorometer (Photon Technology International Inc., USA) and time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis was conducted in a HORIBA Fluorolog-QM (Horiba, Canada). UV–Vis and UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) analyses were performed using Varian Cary 100 Bio and U-3900H Spectrophotometers, respectively. The cyclic voltammogram of CQDs was conducted in a solution of Bu4NPF6 (0.1 M) in DMF as the supporting electrolyte using a standard three-electrode system (BASi Epsilon potentiostat) which consists of glassy carbon as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode.

The stock solution of NTP was prepared by dissolving it into DI water with a final concentration of 480.0 mg L−1. NTP solutions with different concentrations (5.0–300.0 mg L−1) were prepared by diluting the stock solution into DI water. The typical procedures for fluorescence measurement are as follows: a) mixing 2.0 mL of N-CQDs@SiO2 solution completely with 2.0 mL NTP solutions (0–300.0 mg L−1) in a quartz cuvette, and b) recording the fluorescence emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (λex = 360 nm) at room temperature. The assay was triplicated for each sample solution to test the repeatability of the data. pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@SiO2 and its sensing of NTP was studied by varying the pH values (pH 2.0–9.0) of the N-CQDs@SiO2 solution. The fluorescence intensity at 445 nm (λem = 445 nm) was measured at λex = 360 nm in the absence and presence of NTP by adding 100.0 µL of 480.0 mg L−1 NTP solution into the N-CQDs@SiO2 solution.

The selectivity of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor toward NTP was evaluated by adding three other pesticide solutions instead of NTP in a similar way. To verify the influence of other pesticide molecules on the selectivity of the sensor towards NTP, the fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs@SiO2 in the presence of the mixed solution of the four pesticides was also examined. The influence of cations on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@SiO2 was also assessed. 100 mg/L of common metal cations (e.g., Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Zn2+) and heavy metal ions (Pb2+ and Cd2+) were added to the N-CQDs@SiO2 solution (2.0 mL + 2.0 mL), respectively. The resulting mixture was incubated for 2 min and monitored with the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NTP in natural water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Natural water samples including surface water, groundwater, and soil leachate were collected from a local waste management facility (St. John’s, Canada). The natural water samples were first centrifuged for 15 min at 4000 rpm to remove the large particles and suspended soil and then filtered using a 0.22 μM membrane. After that, NTP natural water solutions with different concentrations of NTP were prepared. Fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor by NTP natural water solutions was measured using the same method as that for NTP DI water solutions.

Fig. 1 a shows the HR-TEM images of the synthesized N-CQDs, which indicate that the particle sizes of N-CQDs were predominantly in the range of 5 – 10 nm, and they were essentially spherical. Partial self-passivation could lead to variation in sizes however a distinct spherical morphology (inset of Fig. 1a) was maintained throughout. Surface functional groups of N-CQDs were clarified from the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b), which suggested that N-CQDs were comprised of –OH and –NH groups (broad peaks in 3500–3100 cm−1), carboxyl group with characteristic peaks of CO at 1699 cm−1, CN (1651 cm−1), aromatic CC (1538 cm−1), CH at 1392 cm−1, as well as CN and CO at 1217 and 1050 cm−1, respectively. The results show good agreement with those reported in the open literature for N-CQDs prepared with similar carbon and nitrogen precursors [40–42].

XPS is an important chemical characterization technique for CQDs, which provides detailed information on the elemental composition and the bond properties of the atoms that make up the CQDs. XPS survey spectrum shows that the as-prepared N-CQDs are mainly composed of three elements of C, O, and N (Fig. 1c). In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1d), three deconvoluted BE peaks at 284.8, 286.04, 287.42 eV could be assigned to the sp2 C (CC/CC), the oxidized C (CO and CO) and sp3 bonded C (CN), respectively [43]. Two deconvoluted BE peaks at 399.63 and 400.97 eV corresponding to pyridine N (CN) and amino N (NH) were observed for N 1s from Fig. 1e [43,44]. The two peaks of the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) at 531.18 and 532.21 eV are attributed to the CO and CO groups respectively [42,45].

To scrutinize the optical properties of N-CQDs, UV − vis absorption and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs were investigated. It was seen from Fig. 1g that the as-prepared N-CQDs have a broad absorption peak at 239 nm, which could be identified as the π − π* transition of the aromatic sp2 (CC) bonds. N-CQDs also have an absorption peak at 345 nm, which is due to the n − π* transition of the CO bond [28]. The fluorescence spectra reveal that the N-CQDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 and 445 nm. The inset of Fig. 1g shows the picture of N-CQDs under UV light (395 nm) irradiation in bright blue color. The UV–Vis DRS spectrum (Fig. 1h) was measured and employed to obtain the Tauc plot, from which the bandgap energy of N-CQDs was determined (E g = 2.15 eV).

FT-IR and XPS analyses were performed for the surface functionality and composition of the N-CQDs@SiO2 composite either. From the IR spectrum of N-CQDs@SiO2 illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the presence of vital vibrational modes referring to the SiOSi groups was observed from typical transmittance bands at 451 and 794 cm−1, as well as a very prominent band in the range of 1000–1250 cm−1 [46]. XPS survey spectrum shown in Fig. 2b confirmed that the atomic levels of elements Si and O are tens-fold higher than those of C and N in the composite gel. However, N-CQDs@SiO2 gel demonstrated very similar UV − Vis absorbance, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 2c) as those of N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on fluorescent sensing of NTP
Fluorescent sensing of NTP by N-CQDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs@SiO2 solution in the absence and presence of NTP obtained at excitation of 360 nm were presented in Fig. 4 a -over a concentration range of 0–300.0 mg L−1. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@SiO2 decreased with the increase of NTP concentration without any blue- or red shift. Fig. 4b showed a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity change of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor at 445 nm and concentrations of NTP over the range of 5.0–300.0 mg L−1, which could be described by Stern-Volmer correlation in Eq. (1),
(1)
F 0
/ F
= 1 +
K
SV
c

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@SiO2 in the absence and presence of NTP respectively, K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and c is the concentration of NTP (mg L−1). By linear regression, the K SV value is calculated to be 3.9 L g−1 with a correlation coefficient, R 2, of 0.997. The narrow range of error bars in Fig. 4b indicates stable conditions of the sensor solution and the NTP solution, leading to accurate and reliable results. Using the linear calibration curve at lower concentrations and the standard deviation of intercept, the LOD of the prepared sensor is 1.53 mg L−1 with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.3, which is competitive to the results by other fluorometry-based methods for NTP detection (Table 1 ).

The binding constant (K a) of NTP to the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor could be determined using the below equation [53].
(2)
1
Δ F
=
1
Δ
F max
+
1
Δ
F max
K a
1 c

In Eq. (2), ΔF (F 0-F) is the change of fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs@SiO2 in the presence of NTP; ΔF max is the maximal change of fluorescence intensity, and c is the concentration of NTP solution. By linear regression of 1/ΔF versus 1/c based on Eq. (2) over the concentration range of 5.0–300.0 mg L−1, the K a value of 7.36 L g−1 has been obtained, which indicates a rather strong binding affinity between NTP and N-CQDs@SiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of NTP and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing selectivity of the as-prepared N-CQDs@SiO2 was studied by mixing the N-CQDs@SiO2 gel with individual pesticide solutions (e.g., NTP, IMD, TMX, and ATZ) at three concentration levels. As seen from Fig. 5 a, significant fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs@SiO2 occurred after being immersed in 25.0, 100.0, and 250.0 mg L−1 NTP solutions, leading to a 9.7–50 % reduction in the fluorescence intensity. Because of the similar chemical structures of IMD and NTP, IMD could also quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs@SiO2 by 5.5–12 % at concentrations of 100.0 and 250.0 mg L−1, which are significantly lower than those by NTP at the same concentration levels. However, the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor had no distinctive fluorescent response to TMX and ATZ among the four compounds. The selectivity of N-CQDs@SiO2 toward NTP was also confirmed by the distinct fluorescence responses of N-CQDs@SiO2 to mixtures of pesticide solutions with and without NTP. The mixture solution with NTP caused a significant quenching effect on the sensor, whereas other coexisting interference molecules had no effect on the fluorescence response of N-CQDs@SiO2.

The influence of metal ions on the fluorescence emission of N-CQDs@SiO2 was measured to verify the anti-interference performance of the sensor (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@SiO2 was almost unaffected by Na+ and Ca2+, but slightly quenched (2–5 %) by Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, demonstrating that the constructed sensor had high resistance against different interfering components including metal ions and other pesticides.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra of four pesticides as well as the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs@SiO2 were measured and compared in Fig. 6 a to further understand the sensing selectivity and the probable sensing mechanisms. As can be seen from Fig. 6a, none of the pesticides’ absorbance spectra overlaps with the emission spectrum of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor, ruling out the involvement of FRET in the fluorescence quenching. The UV absorbance spectra of ATZ and TMX do not overlap with the excitation spectrum of the sensor, whereas IMD’s absorbance spectrum marginally overlaps and the NTP’s absorbance spectrum significantly overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the sensor. As such, the fluorescence quenching of the sensor is mainly caused by the IFE, which attenuates the excitation beam when NTP (or IMD) is present. Results from the fluorescence lifetime profiles of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor in the presence and absence of NTP (Fig. 6b) confirm the fluorescence quenching of the sensor by IFE as the fluorescence lifetime is unchanged with the addition of NTP.

To check the involvement of the PET mechanism in the fluorescence quenching, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of the N-CQDs were determined through the measurement of the redox potential from cyclic voltammetry (E red = -0.66 eV, Fig. 6c) and the bandgap energy obtained from UV–Vis DRS analysis (E g = 2.15 eV, Fig. 1h) of N-CQDs based on the following equations [54]:
(3-3)
E
LUMO
= -
E
red
+ 4.4
(3-4)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g

The LUMO and HOMO levels of N-CQDs were found to be −3.74 eV and −5.89 eV respectively, whereas the LUMO level of NTP based on the reported research is −1.55 eV [55]. Photoexcited electrons in the N-CQDs are not energetically allowed to transfer to the LUMO of NTP, thereby ruling out the PET between N-CQDs and NTP (Fig. 6d). Such analysis suggests IFE is the major mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by NTP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of NTP in natural water samples
ce:section.ce:para: We spiked three naturally occurring water samples (surface water, groundwater, and soil leachate) with various NTP concentrations to evaluate the sensor's capacity to detect NTP from natural water samples. The results of calibration curves and recovery rates are presented in Table 2 . All recovery rates are within a reasonable range, showing the sensor's capacity to measure NTP levels in various water samples.

In this work, a novel CQDs-based fluorescent sensor (N-CQDs@SiO2) was prepared and applied for the selective detection of nitenpyram (NTP), a pesticide from the neonicotinoid class that may present in soil and water. Characterization of the synthesized sensor was performed through FTIR, HR-TEM, and XPS analyzers to prove the desired chemical structure and particle size. The as-prepared sensor demonstrated remarkable analytical performance over the NTP concentration range of 5.0 to 300.0 mg L−1 with a low LOD of 1.53 mg L−1. This sensor showed excellent selectivity toward NTP in the presence of other pesticides and metal ions. By comparing the UV–vis absorbance spectra of four pesticides with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs@SiO2 sensor, a significant overlap between the NTP’s absorbance spectrum and the excitation spectrum of the sensor was observed, indicating that fluorescence quenching of the sensor is mainly caused by the IFE. Finally, the prepared sensor was tested for its ability to detect NTP from real water samples, and the results showed acceptable recovery rates.
Masoumeh Rostami: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Baiyu Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yan Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by theNatural Sciences and Engineering Research Councilof Canada (NSERC) under the Discovery Grant (RGPIN-2017-04510).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M.L. Hladik, A.R. Main, D. Goulson, Environmental risks and challenges associated with neonicotinoid insecticides, Environ., Sci. Technol. 52(2018), 3529–3535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fate of selected neonicotinoid insecticides in soil–water systems: Current state of the art and knowledge gaps

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Pietrzak, J. Kania, E. Kmiecik, G. Malina, K. Wątor, Fate of selected neonicotinoid insecticides in soil–water systems: Current state of the art and knowledge gaps, Chemosphere 255 (2020), 126981.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing matrix effects on the heterogeneous photochemistry of neonicotinoid pesticides, dinotefuran and nitenpyram

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Rohrbacher, M.J. Ezell, V. Perraud, B.J. Finlayson-Pitts, Probing matrix effects on the heterogeneous photochemistry of neonicotinoid pesticides, dinotefuran and nitenpyram, ACS Earth SpaceChem.5(2021),1196–1209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mammalian susceptibility to a neonicotinoid insecticide after fetal and early postnatal exposure
